stem: A numerical method has error \( E(h) \approx C\,h^{p} \) for small step size \( h \). On a coarse grid with step size \( h \) its error is \( [[e1]] \); refining to step size \( \dfrac{h}{[[r]]} \) the error drops to \( [[e2]] \). Estimate the order of accuracy \( p \).
solution: Since \( E \approx C h^{p} \), the two errors satisfy \( \dfrac{E(h)}{E(h/[[r]])} = [[r]]^{\,p} \). Thus \( [[r]]^{\,p} = \dfrac{[[e1]]}{[[e2]]} \), giving \( p = \log_{[[r]]}\!\left(\dfrac{[[e1]]}{[[e2]]}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the error ratio, giving \( -p \) instead of \( p \).
This reports the error ratio \( r^{p} \) itself rather than its base-\( r \) logarithm.
This mistakes the grid refinement factor \( r \) for the order of accuracy.
This is off by one above the correct order.
This is off by one below the correct order.
This is off by two from the correct order.

stem: A numerical method is known to have order of accuracy \( p = [[p]] \), so its error obeys \( E(h) \approx C\,h^{p} \). On a grid with step size \( h \), the observed error is \( [[Ecoarse]] \). If the step size is refined to \( \dfrac{h}{[[r]]} \), what error would you expect?
solution: Halving-type refinement scales the error by \( r^{p} \): \( E(h/r) = E(h)/r^{p} \). So the new error is \( \dfrac{[[Ecoarse]]}{[[r]]^{[[p]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies by \( r^{p} \) instead of dividing, growing the error instead of shrinking it.
This only accounts for a single factor of \( r \), ignoring the order \( p \).
This divides by \( p \) instead of \( r^{p} \), confusing the order with the refinement factor.
This uses one extra power of \( r \).
This uses one too few powers of \( r \).
This is off by one from the correct predicted error.

stem: Method A has order of accuracy \( p_1 = [[p1]] \) and Method B has order of accuracy \( p_2 = [[p2]] \); both have error \( E(h) \approx C h^{p} \). Both methods are run with the same step size \( h \) and then both are refined to step size \( \dfrac{h}{[[r]]} \). By what factor does Method A's error shrink MORE than Method B's, i.e. what is \( \dfrac{E_A(h)/E_A(h/[[r]])}{E_B(h)/E_B(h/[[r]])} \)?
solution: Refining by a factor of \( [[r]] \) shrinks Method A's error by \( [[r]]^{p_1} \) and Method B's by \( [[r]]^{p_2} \). The ratio of shrink factors is \( \dfrac{[[r]]^{p_1}}{[[r]]^{p_2}} = [[r]]^{[[p1]]-[[p2]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio, comparing B's shrink to A's instead of A's to B's.
This reports the difference of the orders instead of \( r \) raised to that difference.
This uses one extra power of \( r \).
This uses one too few powers of \( r \).
This mistakes the ratio of the orders themselves for the ratio of shrink factors.
This is off by one from the correct factor.

stem: A numerical method has order of accuracy \( p = [[p]] \), so \( E(h) \approx C h^{p} \). By what factor \( r \) must the step size be reduced (\( h \to h/r \), with \( r > 1 \)) in order to reduce the error by a factor of \( [[Q]] \) (so the new error is \( \dfrac{1}{[[Q]]} \) times the old error)?
solution: Refining by factor \( r \) shrinks the error by \( r^{p} \), so we need \( r^{p} = [[Q]] \), i.e. \( r = [[Q]]^{1/[[p]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides the reduction factor by the order instead of taking a \( p \)-th root.
This subtracts the order from the reduction factor instead of taking a \( p \)-th root.
This multiplies instead of taking a \( p \)-th root.
This is one more than the correct refinement factor.
This is one less than the correct refinement factor.
This squares the correct refinement factor.

stem: A root-finding iteration produces three consecutive absolute errors \( e_0 = [[e0]] \), \( e_1 = [[e1]] \), \( e_2 = [[e2]] \) (no known step-size ratio is given), where the errors satisfy \( e_{n+1} \approx C\,e_n^{\,p} \) for a fixed order of convergence \( p \) and constant \( C \). Using \( p \approx \dfrac{\ln(e_2/e_1)}{\ln(e_1/e_0)} \), estimate \( p \).
solution: Taking logs of both successive error ratios and dividing gives the order of convergence: \( p \approx \dfrac{\ln(e_2/e_1)}{\ln(e_1/e_0)} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts which ratio is the base and which is the argument.
This reverses the order of the successive errors used in each ratio.
This reports the raw ratio \( e_0/e_1 \) instead of the order \( p \).
This reports the raw ratio \( e_1/e_2 \) instead of the order \( p \).
This is off by one above the correct order.
This is off by one below the correct order.
